task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
Cold iron clamps close around Cody's ankle, the snarl of the spring echoing in the hush of the forest. He gasps, the world tilting as the metallic jaws bite into his skin, and his palms scrape the damp earth. Above the trap a faint blue LED blinks, pulsing like a heartbeat in the dim underbrush. The tiny mouse, its bell still jingling, scrambles on the opposite side of the net, eyes wide with terror.  

‘Hold on!’ he shouts, voice cracking, as he claws at the tangled rope. The steel bar that holds the trap is rusted, but it refuses to give. He remembers the knife tucked in his pack, the very blade that freed Marahute’s ropes moments before. With trembling fingers he slides the knife from its sheath, feeling the cold metal against his palm.  

He slices through the thick cord, the blade singing as it parts. The trap’s hinge creaks, the jaws loosening enough for his foot to slip free. A surge of relief floods his chest, but the mouse’s frantic chirps remind him that the danger isn’t over. He pulls the squeaky creature onto his shoulder, the bell ringing louder now, echoing against the trees.  

The blinking LED catches his eye. It’s attached to a small solar-powered transmitter, likely a beacon the poachers use to mark traps. Cody knows the forest well enough to trace the signal. He pockets the mouse, grips his knife tighter, and follows the faint glow deeper into the gully, where the air grows colder and the scent of smoke lingers.  

The gully opens onto a shallow clearing, littered with broken branches and half‑eaten carrion. In the centre stands a crude wooden platform, strung with tangled nets and a battered lantern that flickers with a weak flame. Shadows move behind the nets – shapes of men crouched, checking their traps. Cody’s heart pounds as he realizes he’s stepped onto the poachers’ hideout.  

From behind a fallen log, Cody crouches low, his breath shallow. He watches as one of the men, a scarred fellow with a grizzled beard, pulls out a weather‑worn map and spreads it across the platform. The map is marked with red Xs, each denoting a trapped animal. One X glows faintly, matching the LED he followed.  

He knows he must act fast. Pulling the mouse close, he whispers, ‘We’re getting you out, little buddy.’ He reaches into his pack, pulls out a length of thin vine he’s collected earlier, and begins tying a makeshift rope around the platform’s support beam. His mind races: if he can bring the whole net down, the poachers will be forced to flee, and the trapped creatures might escape.  

Suddenly, a familiar trill slices through the murmur of the poachers. Faloo swoops down from the canopy, his iridescent wings flashing in the dim light. Landing on a low branch, the eagle eyes Cody with a mix of curiosity and urgency. ‘You’re in deep, kid,’ Faloo croaks, ‘but I can help. We need to distract them.’  

Cody nods, swallowing the fear. He tugs the vine tighter, securing it around the beam. Faloo lets out a sharp cry, drawing the poachers’ attention. One of them, the scarred leader, raises his rifle and aims at the soaring bird. The eagle darts, weaving between trees, and in a daring swoop he plucks a loose branch and hurls it into the lantern, extinguishing the flame.  

The sudden darkness causes confusion; the net snaps as the support beam gives way under the weight of the vine and the hasty tug. Nets tumble, ropes coiling like serpents across the ground. Trapped animals—kangaroos, wombats, even a koala—are freed as their cages fall open. The poachers scramble, tripping over each other, shouting curses.  

Cody scoops up the trembling mouse, cradling it against his chest. Faloo circles overhead, his keen eyes spotting a young wallaby tangled in a snare. With a powerful beat of his wings, the eagle drops a sturdy branch, which Cody grabs and uses as a lever to free the wallaby. The freed creatures scatter, bounding away toward the safety of the bush.  

The scarred leader, eyes blazing, shouts, ‘Get that eagle!’ He lunges toward Faloo, but the eagle is already ascending, his massive wings beating a thunderous rhythm. Behind him, the remaining poacher darts to the fallen lantern, trying to salvage what they can, but the fire is out and the night swallows their hopes.  

Cody grabs the map, feeling the torn edges beneath his fingers. He sees several red Xs far beyond this clearing, each representing a snare waiting for unsuspecting wildlife. Determined, he folds the map and tucks it into his pack. ‘We have to warn the others,’ he whispers to Faloo, who nods, his head tilting as if understanding every word.  

The pair makes a swift retreat, slipping through the tangled woods. As they emerge near a wide river, the water roars, swollen from recent storms. A rickety wooden bridge spans the current, its planks warped and slick. Cody steps cautiously, the mouse clinging to his shirt, while Faloo watches from the bank, ready to swoop if needed.  

Cody balances on the narrow plank, heart hammering. Halfway across, a gust pushes the bridge, causing it to sway dangerously. Below, the river churns, promising a swift, cold plunge. Just then, the scarred poacher appears at the opposite bank, shouting, ‘There’s a kid!’ He lunges, brandishing a crude spear, determined to recapture his prize.  

Cody’s reflexes kick in. He slides his foot onto the next board, uses his momentum to vault over the gap, and lands on the far side with a thud. The poacher’s spear clatters harmlessly onto the bridge. Faloo circles high, then dives, snatching the spear mid‑air and dropping it into the river, where it disappears with a splash.  

With the immediate threat gone, Cody and Faloo press onward, following the river upstream. The water guides them to a narrow canyon where a faint plume of smoke curls from a hidden clearing. Peering through scrub, they spot a makeshift encampment: canvas tents, rusted firearms, and a crude cage holding a shimmering silver platypus. The sign above the cage reads ‘McLeach’s Prize – $5000 today.’

 Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter  . 

[Requirement] Write an article based on this summary. The article must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .

response:
Actress Taraji P. Henson, who has become a vocal advocate for social justice, disclosed this week that her 16‑year‑old son, Marcel, was subjected to what she described as a racial profiling incident on the campus of the University of Southern California. According to Henson, the incident occurred last month when university police officers stopped Marcel near the campus library, questioned him aggressively, and demanded to see identification despite his clear status as a student in good standing. The actress, who has previously used her platform to highlight inequities in policing, said the encounter left her son shaken and prompted her to demand accountability from the institution.  

The university’s public safety chief, Deputy Director Marcus Alvarez, publicly acknowledged that he himself has been a victim of racial profiling during his years of service in multiple jurisdictions. Alvarez told reporters that an officer once stopped him on a routine traffic stop, questioned his motives, and detained him for longer than necessary simply because of his ethnicity. “I have lived the experience that many of our students and community members describe,” he said. “When you have personally endured that kind of bias, it becomes impossible to ignore it within our own campus.” He added that the department is reviewing its training protocols and will cooperate fully with any external investigation.  

John Thomas, a prominent local civil‑rights attorney and longtime friend of the Henson family, announced that he will commission an independent review of the incident. Thomas said he plans to hire a third‑party forensic investigator to examine body‑camera footage, officer dispatch logs, and the university’s internal reports. “The community deserves transparency,” Thomas told a press conference on Tuesday. “If the police department and the university are serious about restoring trust, they must allow an unbiased party to assess what really happened and recommend concrete reforms.” He also urged the university to provide support services for students who feel targeted or unsafe on campus.  

Over the past several years, USC has been under increasing scrutiny for its handling of race‑related complaints. In 2021, a group of students filed a class‑action lawsuit alleging that the campus police disproportionately stopped and searched Black and Latino students, citing statistical data that showed a 2.5‑to‑1 disparity compared with white students. The lawsuit was settled in 2023 for $2.3 million, and the university pledged to overhaul its policing policies, introduce implicit‑bias training, and establish a civilian oversight board. Yet activists claim progress has been slow, pointing to recent incidents such as the stop of Marcel Henson as evidence that old habits persist.  

The student government association (SGA) issued a statement on Wednesday urging the university to expedite the independent investigation and to make the findings public within a thirty‑day window. SGA President Maya Patel wrote that “our campus cannot be a place where any student feels they will be singled out because of the color of their skin.” She called for the implementation of a transparent reporting system, mandatory de‑escalation training for all officers, and the appointment of a permanent community liaison tasked with bridging gaps between law enforcement and the diverse student population.  

If the independent probe uncovers misconduct, the university could face renewed lawsuits and potential federal civil‑rights investigations. Legal scholars note that the Department of Education’s Office for Civil Rights (OCR) has the authority to examine institutional compliance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act, which prohibits discrimination on the basis of race in programs receiving federal funding. A finding of non‑compliance could jeopardize the university’s federal financial aid eligibility and trigger mandatory corrective action plans.  

In a follow‑up interview with a national news outlet, Henson said her son’s experience “mirrored too many stories we have heard from Black families across the country.” She expressed disappointment that a campus, which prides itself on diversity and inclusion, would allow what she termed “a micro‑aggression that escalated into an intimidation tactic.” Henson also announced that she will partner with local advocacy groups to host a town‑hall meeting on campus next month, inviting students, faculty, law‑enforcement representatives, and community leaders to discuss concrete steps toward rebuilding trust.  

The incident arrives at a time when policing practices at colleges nationwide are under intense examination. In recent months, universities such as Columbia, UCLA, and Boston University have faced protests after students reported being stopped for minor infractions while on their way to classes, prompting administrations to reevaluate campus security policies. Civil‑rights organizations argue that the lack of uniform standards for university police forces creates a patchwork of accountability that can enable discriminatory behavior.  

As the investigation proceeds, all eyes remain on USC’s leadership to see whether the promised reforms will move beyond rhetoric and result in measurable change. For Marcel Henson and other students who have felt targeted, the process represents not only a quest for justice but also a test of the university’s commitment to protecting the rights of every member of its community.  

The Los Angeles Police Department’s (LAPD) police union, represented by President Carlos Ramirez, released a brief comment acknowledging the incident but emphasizing that officers are trained to act on reasonable suspicion. Ramirez stated, “Our members are committed to serving the community with professionalism and respect, and any claim of bias will be taken seriously and investigated thoroughly.” However, critics argue that such statements often overlook systemic issues and call for independent oversight rather than internal reviews. In response, the city’s mayor’s office announced that the mayor’s office will monitor the outcome of the USC investigation and will convene a task force to assess policing practices at all municipal‑funded university security agencies.  

Following the public outcry, a coalition of student organizations organized a peaceful rally on the campus quad, drawing more than 1,200 participants. Speakers included freshman activist Jamal Washington, who urged the administration to “listen to the lived realities of Black and brown students,” and faculty member Dr. Elaine Chen, who highlighted research showing that perceived over‑policing can adversely affect mental health and academic performance. The rally concluded with a moment of silence for victims of police‑related trauma and a pledge to submit a petition demanding the creation of an independent civilian review board with binding authority to investigate future complaints.

The actress, who has previously used her platform to highlight inequities in policing, said the encounter left her son shaken and prompted her to demand accountability from the institution.

Alvarez told reporters that an officer once stopped him on a routine traffic stop, questioned his motives, and detained him for longer than necessary simply because of his ethnicity. “I have lived the experience that many of our students and community members describe,” he said. “When you have personally endured that kind of bias, it becomes impossible to ignore it within our own campus.” He added that the department is reviewing its training protocols and will cooperate fully with any external investigation.  

John Thomas, a prominent local civil‑rights attorney and longtime friend of the Henson family, announced that he will commission an independent review of the incident.
